reference_answer: That Fraser had seen Harrowgate's wholesale pricing sheet and that it was given to him by 'your account manager — the one who handles the Midwest territory'
That someone from Harrowgate gave him a document. Fraser explicitly states he did not say it was a pricing sheet and that Ashton may have misunderstood.
A business card and a copy of the public product catalog, which is available on Harrowgate's website and does not contain pricing information
A document that he thought was a product catalog. He says he flipped through it briefly and doesn't remember seeing prices.

She saw Mehta talking to several people including someone who might have been Fraser, and saw Mehta give out the product catalog. She did not see Mehta give out any pricing document. But she was not with Mehta every moment.

No evidence that the wholesale pricing schedule was accessed, downloaded, printed, or emailed from Mehta's company laptop or email during January 1 through February 10. However, shared-drive logs covering the conference period were purged before the review began.
Three points: (a) Fraser's statement is self-serving; (b) Tan was not with Mehta at all times; (c) the purged logs cannot affirmatively exclude the possibility of disclosure
She notes (in German) that Fraser's and Ashton's statements do not agree on whether Fraser described the document as a pricing sheet or just a document. She calls this discrepancy material.
February 7, 2026 (corrected from initially stated February 5)
January 1 through February 10, 2026 (corrected from initially stated February 15)
The allegation is not substantiated. The available evidence does not support a finding that Mehta disclosed proprietary pricing data.
Director of Compliance Yusuf Okonkwo, as the investigator
No — the Panel upheld Finding 5. The allegation remains unsubstantiated. The Panel added a supplemental finding recommending a new policy, but did not change the substantive determination.

The Panel recommends that Harrowgate implement a policy requiring conference materials distributed to external parties to be pre-approved. This is prospective and does not imply Mehta violated any existing policy.
No — the Panel explicitly states the recommendation is prospective and does not imply that Mehta violated any existing policy
General Counsel Victoria Lam, CFO André Beaumont, and outside counsel Dr. Mika Oshiro
No — Okonkwo conducted the investigation. The Review Panel is a separate three-person body that reviews the file independently.
The Panel explicitly declined to find either (a) that the disclosure occurred or (b) that the disclosure did not occur. The evidence is insufficient to support either conclusion.
No — Finding 6 explicitly states that the finding does not exonerate Mehta. It states that the allegation cannot be substantiated on the current evidence.
The shared-drive access logs covering the conference period (January 20-22) were purged before the investigation began on February 12. The logs are retained for only 30 days.

Whether Fraser identified the document as a pricing sheet. Ashton says Fraser said he 'had seen our wholesale pricing sheet.' Fraser says he told Ashton that 'someone from Harrowgate gave me a document' without identifying it as a pricing sheet.
Secondhand — Ashton is reporting what Fraser allegedly told him at a dinner. Fraser's own firsthand account differs from Ashton's version.

Approximately — Mehta says she gave a public product catalog. Fraser says he received a document he thought was a product catalog. Both describe the same type of document, though Fraser is less specific.
It partially supports it — Tan saw Mehta distribute the product catalog. It also contains a limitation: Tan was not with Mehta at all times, so her testimony cannot cover Mehta's every interaction.

No — Portillo found no evidence on the laptop, but notes that the pricing schedule is also accessible from the shared sales drive, and the shared-drive logs for the conference period were already purged. The laptop finding is necessary but not sufficient to exclude access via other means.
No — it is Ashton's characterization of Fraser's motive. The investigation report does not adopt this characterization as a finding.

Possibly — the logs are retained for 30 days. The conference was January 20-22. The investigation began February 12. The 30-day retention would have expired around February 19-21. If the investigation had accessed the logs before that date, they would have been available. But the complaint was not filed until February 10.
No other witness confirms or denies this. It is Ashton's statement. The document does not establish whether other Harrowgate employees also cover the Midwest territory.

It means the review covered 5 fewer days than initially reported. However, the key period (the January conference) is within both the original and corrected ranges. The correction does not change Portillo's conclusion.
No — the dinner date establishes when Ashton learned of the alleged disclosure, not whether the disclosure occurred. Both dates are after the conference.

Unknown — the official finding is 'not substantiated,' which means the evidence is insufficient to determine whether the disclosure occurred. The Review Panel explicitly declined to find that it did or did not occur.
Not substantiated — the allegation was investigated, the finding was upheld by the Review Panel, and no disciplinary action was taken. This is not the same as exoneration.
Finding 5 (not substantiated) was upheld by the Review Panel. Ashton's objection was considered but did not change the outcome. The case is closed with the 'not substantiated' finding.
No — the supplemental finding recommends implementing such a policy, which implies no such policy currently exists. The recommendation is prospective.

The evidence supports that a public product catalog was given. Both Mehta and Fraser describe a product catalog. Neither describes a pricing sheet. Ashton's secondhand account that Fraser called it a pricing sheet is contradicted by Fraser's own statement.

The document does not make a determination about Fraser's reliability. Ashton argues Fraser is self-serving. The investigation and Review Panel note the discrepancy between Ashton's and Fraser's accounts but do not assess credibility beyond noting the discrepancy.

The Review Panel recommended requiring pre-approval of conference materials distributed to external parties. This is a recommendation, not yet an implemented policy. The document does not record whether the recommendation was adopted.
Four: English (Ashton, Mehta, Portillo), French (Fraser), Indonesian (Tan), and German (Estrada)

1) Whether the disclosure actually occurred (explicitly unresolved by the Panel); 2) What was on the shared-drive logs during the conference period (purged, unrecoverable); 3) Whether the recommended pre-approval policy was implemented (not recorded); 4) Whether other Harrowgate employees also cover the Midwest territory (not established)

Yes — Finding 5 is based on available evidence and explicitly acknowledges the evidentiary gap. New evidence could change the finding. However, the supplemental recommendation would remain valid as a prospective policy change regardless of any new finding.
